name: Zomato
tagline: Food delivery and restaurant discovery for India
founders: Deepinder Goyal, Pankaj Chaddah
problem: Finding restaurants, reading menus, and ordering food online was fragmented and unreliable in India. Restaurant discovery was word-of-mouth and menus were not accessible digitally. Customers had no visibility into delivery times, order tracking, or food quality ratings.
keyFeatures: Real-time food delivery with live GPS tracking
Restaurant discovery with ratings, reviews, and menus
Zomato Gold / Pro subscription for dining discounts
Hyperpure B2B ingredient supply to restaurants
businessModel: Commission on food orders (20-25%), advertising from restaurants, Zomato Pro subscriptions, and B2B ingredient supply via Hyperpure
growthStory: Started as Foodiebay in 2008 scanning restaurant menus in Delhi NCR, Zomato pivoted to food delivery in 2015 after seeing Swiggy's traction. It went public in July 2021 at a $8.6B valuation — India's first major internet IPO. After a rough 2022 with losses, Zomato achieved its first profitable quarter in Q2 FY24, vindicating its focus on unit economics over growth-at-all-costs. Acquiring Blinkit in 2022 for ₹4,447 crore diversified it into quick commerce.
keyMetrics: Over 300 million registered users across 800+ cities
Blinkit contributing 12%+ of total revenue by Q4 FY24
First profitable quarter: Q2 FY24 with ₹36 crore PAT
pmLessons: Unit economics matter more than GMV: Zomato's journey to profitability showed that delivery radius, order value, and delivery density per area are more important levers than user count in logistics-heavy businesses.

Portfolio expansion via acquisition is faster than build: Instead of building quick commerce from scratch, Zomato bought Blinkit — a PM lesson that sometimes a strategic acquisition beats a 2-year internal product build.

Tier-2/3 cities require localised pricing: Zomato's average order values in metros are 2x those in smaller cities. PMs must segment pricing, delivery fees, and restaurant partners by city tier rather than applying a single national strategy.

name: Swiggy
tagline: Instant food delivery from your favourite restaurants
founders: Sriharsha Majety, Nandan Reddy, Rahul Jaimini
problem: Ordering food from local restaurants required phone calls, cash-only payments, and unreliable delivery by restaurant staff. There was no standardised platform providing real-time tracking, consistent delivery SLAs, or digital payments for food orders across India.
keyFeatures: Food delivery with 30-45 min SLA and real-time tracking
Instamart for 10-20 minute grocery delivery
Swiggy One subscription for free deliveries and discounts
Dineout integration for table reservations and dining offers
businessModel: Commission from restaurant partners (20-30%), delivery fees, Swiggy One subscription revenue, and advertising from FMCG brands on the platform
growthStory: Launched in Bengaluru in 2014 with a focus on owning the entire delivery experience — unlike Zomato which initially relied on restaurant delivery staff. This operational control became Swiggy's moat. It expanded to 500+ cities and launched Instamart in 2020 to capture the quick commerce wave. Swiggy filed for its IPO in 2024, listing at a valuation of ~$11.3B. The company has been investing heavily in Instamart dark stores as quick commerce becomes a key battleground.
keyMetrics: Instamart operates 700+ dark stores across 40+ cities
Over 200 million registered users on the platform
pmLessons: Owning logistics is a product decision: By hiring their own delivery fleet instead of relying on restaurants, Swiggy could guarantee SLAs and build trust — a key PM insight that controlling the full stack creates differentiated customer experience.

Category expansion requires dark store density planning: Instamart's success depends on placing dark stores within 2-3 km of high-demand pin codes. PMs in quick commerce must think in terms of geographic micro-segmentation, not city-level rollout.

Subscription reduces churn in high-frequency verticals: Swiggy One increases order frequency by removing per-order friction. For Indian consumers who are delivery-fee sensitive, a flat subscription changes the psychological calculus of ordering.

name: PhonePe
tagline: India's most trusted UPI payments app
founders: Sameer Nigam, Rahul Chari, Burzin Engineer
problem: Digital payments in India were complex, app-specific, and required knowing the recipient's bank account details. Cash dominated transactions even among smartphone users because no unified, instant, bank-agnostic payment layer existed until UPI.
keyFeatures: UPI-based instant peer-to-peer and merchant payments
PhonePe Switch — mini-app marketplace for third-party services
Insurance, mutual funds, and gold investment products
Indus Appstore — an alternative Android app store for India
businessModel: Zero-MDR on UPI transactions (regulatory mandate); monetises through financial services (insurance, mutual funds), payment gateway fees for business accounts, and the Indus Appstore
growthStory: PhonePe was founded in 2015 and acquired by Flipkart in 2016, which gave it instant distribution to millions of Flipkart users. It was early to integrate UPI at launch and captured 47%+ market share of UPI transactions by volume — making it India's #1 payments app. Spun off from Flipkart in 2023 with a $12B valuation, PhonePe is now building a financial services superapp and launched the Indus Appstore to challenge Google Play's dominance in India.
keyMetrics: 47%+ market share of UPI transaction volume (2024)
Merchant network of 37 million+ across India
pmLessons: Distribution via parent ecosystem is a massive PM shortcut: PhonePe's early growth was turbocharged by Flipkart's existing user base. PMs should evaluate strategic partnerships and acquisition channels as a legitimate go-to-market lever rather than purely organic growth.

Zero pricing can be a regulatory moat: UPI's zero-MDR mandate killed monetisation but also eliminated any reason for merchants to resist adoption. Understanding the regulatory environment and designing business models around it is a critical Indian PM skill.

The superapp strategy works when trust is already established: PhonePe can cross-sell insurance and mutual funds because users already trust it for daily payments. PM lesson: establish trust in a high-frequency, low-risk use case before expanding into high-consideration products.

name: Paytm
tagline: Payments, banking, and financial services for all Indians
founders: Vijay Shekhar Sharma
problem: Millions of Indians lacked access to formal banking and digital payment infrastructure. Mobile recharges, utility bill payments, and small-ticket transactions were cash-heavy and required physical presence. There was no simple digital wallet for the feature-phone and early-smartphone era.
keyFeatures: Paytm Wallet for stored-value digital payments
QR-code based merchant payment acceptance
Paytm Payments Bank for savings accounts and debit cards
Paytm Money for stock trading and mutual fund investments
businessModel: Payment gateway fees, merchant subscription fees for Soundbox devices, financial services commissions (insurance, loans, investments), and advertising on the platform
growthStory: Paytm started as a mobile recharge company in 2010 and skyrocketed after demonetisation in November 2016, adding 7 million new users per day as cash became scarce. It listed on the NSE in November 2021 at India's largest-ever IPO of ₹18,300 crore but crashed 27% on listing day. Paytm faced a severe RBI regulatory action against Paytm Payments Bank in January 2024, restricting its banking operations. The episode is a case study in regulatory risk for fintech PMs.
keyMetrics: Over 100 million monthly active users (pre-2024 regulatory action)
6 million+ merchants with Paytm Soundbox devices
First profitable quarter achieved in Q2 FY24
pmLessons: Regulatory risk is existential in fintech: Paytm's RBI action on its Payments Bank showed that no matter how large the product, non-compliance with financial regulators can undo years of growth overnight. PMs in fintech must make compliance a first-class product requirement.

Being first creates a mental-model advantage: 'Paytm karo' entered Indian vocabulary — when your product becomes a verb, you've achieved brand recognition that's nearly impossible to replicate. Early market entry in digital payments rewarded Paytm with a cultural footprint.

Demonetisation was a macro tailwind that PMs cannot engineer: Paytm's viral growth post-2016 is a reminder that product-market fit can be dramatically accelerated by external events. The lesson is to be operationally ready to scale rapidly when unexpected macro shifts align with your product.

name: CRED
tagline: Rewarding India's most creditworthy citizens
founders: Kunal Shah
problem: Creditworthy Indians with high CIBIL scores had no platform tailored to their financial behaviour. Credit card bill payments were boring, rewards were fragmented across issuers, and there was no community or lifestyle platform built around financial responsibility.
keyFeatures: Credit card bill payment with CRED Coins rewards
CRED Store — curated premium lifestyle marketplace
CRED Cash — short-term personal loans for members
CRED Mint — peer-to-peer lending and investment product
businessModel: Lending revenue from CRED Cash, merchant commissions on CRED Store purchases, advertising from premium brands, and financial products distribution fees
growthStory: CRED launched in 2018 with a radical thesis: build a closed community of credit-worthy Indians (CIBIL 750+) and monetise their high spending power. Kunal Shah's 'delta 4 theory' — that products creating an irreversible behaviour shift achieve defensibility — guided the product philosophy. CRED became famous for its quirky, meme-worthy IPL ads, reaching a $6.4B valuation by 2021. The platform has faced scrutiny over its path to profitability but continues to expand its financial services stack.
keyMetrics: Over 13 million members on the platform (CIBIL 750+ gate)
~₹4 lakh crore in credit card bills paid via CRED annually
pmLessons: Exclusivity drives desire: By restricting membership to CIBIL 750+ users, CRED made the platform aspirational. This is a counter-intuitive PM lesson — deliberately limiting your TAM can increase perceived value and organic word-of-mouth in premium segments.

Rewards change behaviour before monetisation follows: CRED coins gamified bill payments and created a habit loop before any monetisation was visible. PMs should consider how reward mechanisms can drive retention while the business model matures in the background.

India's creditworthy segment is a distinct product opportunity: The top 3-4% of India's credit card holders represent a underserved premium market with very different needs from mass-market fintech users. PMs should think carefully about whether their product serves 'India' or 'Bharat' — they require entirely different strategies.

name: Meesho
tagline: India's most affordable social commerce platform
founders: Vidit Aatrey, Sanjeev Barnwal
problem: Hundreds of millions of price-sensitive Indians in tier-2, 3 and 4 cities and rural areas could not afford or trust the product catalogue on platforms like Amazon and Flipkart. Small-town entrepreneurs — especially women — had no digital means to run a reselling business.
keyFeatures: Zero-commission marketplace for sellers (0% commission model)
WhatsApp-native product sharing for resellers
Vernacular product listings in 10+ Indian languages
Cash-on-delivery and easy return policy for first-time online shoppers
businessModel: Logistics and fulfilment fees, advertising from sellers, and payment processing fees. Moved to 0% commission in 2021 to drive seller supply-side growth
growthStory: Originally a reseller platform where homemakers could share products on WhatsApp and earn commissions, Meesho pivoted in 2021 to a full marketplace model serving Bharat users. It raised $570M at a $4.9B valuation in 2021. By eliminating seller commissions, Meesho attracted a flood of small businesses and offered lower prices than Amazon/Flipkart. It became the most downloaded shopping app in India in 2022, displacing incumbents by going deep into non-metro India.
keyMetrics: 1.3 million+ active sellers on the platform
75%+ orders from tier-2 and beyond cities
Most downloaded shopping app in India (2022 Google Play)
pmLessons: Bharat is not a smaller version of metro India: Meesho's users speak Hindi, Odia, and Tamil; pay via COD; and shop on 2G-3G connections. PMs building for Bharat must independently research vernacular UX, low-bandwidth performance, and COD-first payment flows rather than porting metro assumptions.

Zero commission is a supply-side growth hack: By removing seller fees, Meesho flooded the platform with small suppliers who listed products unavailable on premium platforms. This is a PM lesson in using pricing architecture as a supply acquisition strategy.

WhatsApp as a distribution channel is underrated: Meesho's resellers used WhatsApp as a storefront, making organic sharing the primary distribution engine. PMs in India should design for WhatsApp-native sharing flows, not just in-app virality.

name: Zepto
tagline: 10-minute grocery delivery, delivered
founders: Aadit Palicha, Kaivalya Vohra
problem: Indian consumers needed everyday groceries urgently — a forgotten onion, a last-minute snack — but even the fastest delivery options took 30-45 minutes. Offline kirana stores lacked online discoverability and consistent inventory, while e-grocery apps couldn't promise sub-15-minute delivery.
keyFeatures: 10-minute delivery from hyperlocal dark stores
Zepto Cafe for snacks, beverages, and cooked food
Zepto Pass subscription for free deliveries
Real-time inventory tracking and instant substitution suggestions
businessModel: Delivery fees and platform fees, Zepto Pass subscription, advertising from FMCG brands on-platform, and private label products with higher margins
growthStory: Founded by two Stanford dropouts aged 19, Zepto launched in Mumbai in 2021 and scaled to 10 cities within 18 months. It raised $200M in 2022 and achieved unicorn status. By 2024, Zepto had raised over $1.4 billion cumulatively and was aggressively competing with Blinkit and Swiggy Instamart. It became the fastest Indian startup to reach $1B GMV run-rate. The company's focus on hyperlocal dark store density and operational efficiency differentiated it in a fiercely competitive market.
keyMetrics: Unicorn status achieved in May 2023 ($1.4B valuation)
10-minute median delivery time maintained at scale
pmLessons: In quick commerce, the product IS the logistics: Zepto's 10-minute promise is not a marketing claim — it's a product architecture decision requiring dark store placement within 1.5-2 km of demand clusters, curated SKU lists of ~7,000 items (vs 30,000 on Amazon), and dedicated pickers. PMs here are supply chain designers.

SKU curation is a product decision with P&L impact: Zepto deliberately limits its catalogue to fast-moving items that enable high inventory turns. Over-indexing on assortment breadth reduces dark store efficiency. PMs must balance customer satisfaction with operational unit economics.

Speed is the feature, not a footnote: In a price-sensitive market, convenience at a premium is a hard sell. Zepto succeeded by making 10 minutes the headline feature rather than competing on price, showing that a category-defining promise can create a new consumer behaviour.

name: Groww
tagline: Simple, commission-free investing for every Indian
founders: Lalit Keshre, Harsh Jain, Neeraj Singh, Ishan Bansal
problem: Investing in mutual funds and stocks in India required visiting a bank, filling physical forms, and dealing with complex financial jargon. Young, first-time investors were intimidated by incumbents like traditional brokers and wealth managers who focused on high-net-worth clients.
keyFeatures: Commission-free direct mutual fund investments
Stocks and IPO investing via a simplified mobile interface
SIP (Systematic Investment Plan) automation with reminders
Gold, US stocks, and fixed deposits on a single platform
businessModel: Brokerage fees on stock trades (₹20 per order flat fee), F&O trading fees, distribution fees on financial products, and lending products
growthStory: Groww launched in 2016 as a direct mutual fund platform and grew slowly until the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 triggered a retail investing boom in India. From 2 million users in 2020, it exploded to 20 million by 2021. It expanded into stocks and became the top investing app in India by new demat account openings, surpassing Zerodha. Groww became a unicorn in 2021 with a $1B valuation and hit $3B by 2022. Its simplified UX for first-time investors became its key differentiator.
keyMetrics: India's #1 app for new demat account openings (2023-24)
Monthly SIP book exceeding ₹1,000 crore on platform
pmLessons: Simplicity is a product strategy for first-time users: Groww's onboarding removed every piece of jargon and reduced investment to 3 taps. For a market where most users have never invested before, a PM's job is to eliminate intimidation, not showcase feature depth.

Macro tailwinds can collapse years of runway into months: The 2020 pandemic retail investing boom did for Groww what 5 years of marketing could not. PMs should identify macro catalysts that could accelerate adoption and be operationally prepared to absorb sudden growth spikes.

The first investment product is a gateway: Mutual funds built the habit; stocks and F&O deepened monetisation. PM lesson: use a low-barrier entry product to build trust, then cross-sell higher-value products to activated users.

name: Nykaa
tagline: Beauty and wellness for every Indian woman
founders: Falguni Nayar
problem: Indian women had limited access to authentic, diverse beauty products beyond what was available in local markets. Online beauty was dominated by counterfeit products, and there was no trusted platform offering curated international and domestic beauty brands alongside honest editorial content.
keyFeatures: Curated marketplace for 4,000+ beauty and skincare brands
Nykaa Beauty — private label brand with 500+ SKUs
NykaaMan platform for men's grooming products
Content-commerce integration: tutorials, beauty guides, and influencer reviews
businessModel: Product sales on owned inventory model (carries inventory), private label Nykaa Beauty margins, advertising from brand partners, and offline stores
growthStory: Founded by ex-Kotak banker Falguni Nayar at age 49, Nykaa chose to carry its own inventory — a capital-intensive but quality-control-focused approach. It listed on Indian bourses in November 2021 with a market cap of ₹1 lakh crore and became one of India's few profitable tech unicorns at IPO. Nykaa Fashion diversified beyond beauty. The founding story — a middle-aged woman disrupting beauty retail — itself became a marketing asset, connecting with Indian women across age groups.
keyMetrics: Market cap of ₹1 lakh crore at IPO (November 2021)
4,000+ brands and 2.5 million+ SKUs on platform
pmLessons: Inventory ownership is a product quality signal: Unlike marketplace-only models, Nykaa's decision to hold inventory and control fulfillment eliminated counterfeit risk — a critical trust signal in the beauty category where product authenticity is paramount. PMs must assess when vertical integration serves the customer promise.

Content-to-commerce is uniquely powerful for discovery-driven categories: Beauty tutorials and reviews on Nykaa's platform drive purchase intent without paid advertising. PMs in lifestyle categories should invest in content production as a retention and discovery mechanism, not just paid acquisition.

A founder's personal brand can substitute millions in marketing: Falguni Nayar's inspiring story resonated with Indian women, giving Nykaa free PR and emotional connection. PMs should identify authentic brand narratives that can substitute early-stage marketing spend.

name: Lenskart
tagline: Redefining eyewear for a billion Indians
founders: Peyush Bansal, Amit Chaudhary, Sumeet Kapahi
problem: India has one of the world's highest rates of uncorrected vision — over 500 million Indians need glasses but the market was dominated by unorganised local opticians with poor selection, no price transparency, and no virtual try-on capability. Quality branded eyewear was expensive and inaccessible in smaller cities.
keyFeatures: 3D virtual try-on using phone camera AR
Home eye check-up service with trained optometrists
Prescription-linked frame and lens recommendations
1,000+ offline stores for omni-channel fulfilment
businessModel: Direct-to-consumer eyewear sales online and offline, premium lens upgrades (blue light, anti-glare), brand licensing, and B2B corporate eye care programs
growthStory: Lenskart started as an online-only retailer in 2010 and recognised that Indian consumers needed to try glasses before buying — pivoting aggressively to an omni-channel model from 2015. SoftBank Vision Fund invested $275M in 2019, validating the thesis. Lenskart became profitable and expanded internationally to Singapore and the Middle East. Its Shark Tank India appearance by Peyush Bansal dramatically increased brand awareness. By 2023, it reached a $4.5B valuation.
keyMetrics: 2,000+ physical stores across India, Singapore, and Middle East
40% of orders fulfilled through offline stores
pmLessons: For high-consideration purchases, AR is a conversion tool not a gimmick: Lenskart's 3D try-on reduced returns and increased confidence for first-time online eyewear buyers. PMs should identify where in the funnel uncertainty causes drop-off and design features that directly address that specific anxiety.

Omni-channel is a product requirement when category trust is low: Indian consumers needed to hold, try, and verify glasses with an optometrist. The PM lesson is that purely digital distribution fails when the product has a sensory or clinical verification component.

Offline stores as CAC tools: Lenskart's physical stores often function as discovery and trust-building touchpoints that drive repeat online purchases. PMs must measure the cross-channel attribution correctly — a store visit that converts to an online order is still the store's win.

name: Razorpay
tagline: The complete payments and banking platform for businesses
founders: Harshil Mathur, Shashank Kumar
problem: Indian businesses and startups struggled with fragmented, expensive, and developer-unfriendly payment gateway options. Integration required weeks of effort, success rates were below 90%, and there was no unified dashboard for managing payments, payroll, and banking in one place.
keyFeatures: Payment gateway with 100+ payment methods and 95%+ success rates
RazorpayX — neo-banking and business current accounts
Razorpay Payroll for automated salary disbursement and compliance
Instant refunds and real-time payment analytics dashboard
businessModel: Transaction fees (1.5-2% per transaction), subscription fees for advanced features, float income on business accounts, and lending revenue from Razorpay Capital
growthStory: Started from YCombinator in 2015 as a payment gateway, Razorpay expanded into neo-banking (RazorpayX) and payroll. It grew to process ₹10 lakh crore ($125B) in annual transactions by 2023. Razorpay has been expanding into Southeast Asia and acquired Ezetap (payment terminal company) for offline payments. It became India's most valuable payments startup for businesses with a $7.5B valuation.
keyMetrics: ₹10 lakh crore ($125B+) in annual payment processing volume
95%+ payment success rate across all methods
pmLessons: Developer experience is a B2B product's first moat: Razorpay's clean API documentation and 5-minute integration flow became a legendary developer experience in India. PMs building developer tools must treat docs, sandbox environments, and SDKs as first-class product features.

Success rate is the most important fintech metric: A 1% improvement in payment success rate on ₹10 lakh crore of volume means billions of rupees in saved transactions. PMs in payments should obsess over technical reliability metrics as much as feature velocity.

Bundling banking with payments increases switching costs: By offering current accounts, payroll, and loans alongside the gateway, Razorpay created a sticky ecosystem where switching one product means losing benefits across all others — a classic product bundling strategy for B2B.

name: Ola
tagline: Your ride, anytime, anywhere in India
founders: Bhavish Aggarwal, Ankit Bhati
problem: Hailing a cab in Indian cities was unreliable, price-opaque, and often required negotiating with drivers. Auto-rickshaws and taxis lacked fixed rates, GPS tracking, or cashless payment options, making urban mobility a daily source of frustration for millions.
keyFeatures: App-based cab booking with upfront pricing and GPS tracking
Ola Auto and Ola Bike for affordable intra-city commutes
Ola Electric — EV two-wheeler manufacturing and sales
Ola Money wallet integration for in-app payments
businessModel: Commission from driver-partners (20-25%), surge pricing during peak demand, Ola Electric vehicle sales and subscriptions, and advertising
growthStory: Ola launched in Mumbai in 2010 and became India's leading ride-hailing platform, competing fiercely with Uber. It navigated complex city-by-city RTO regulations, integrated autos and bikes for the mass market, and expanded to the UK, Australia, and New Zealand. Bhavish Aggarwal pivoted to Ola Electric in 2017, building India's largest EV scooter factory ('Futurefactory') in Tamil Nadu. Ola Electric IPO'd in August 2024. The parent OLA Cabs business has faced challenges from driver-partner disputes and competition.
keyMetrics: Ola Electric: 3rd largest EV company in India by volumes (2023)
Ola Electric IPO valuation of ~$4.5 billion (2024)
pmLessons: Regulatory navigation is a product competency in mobility: Every Indian city has different auto, taxi, and bike-taxi rules. Ola had to build city-specific compliance playbooks into its operations — PMs in regulated mobility must treat legal compliance as a feature requirement, not an afterthought.

Category diversification (EV) requires a completely different product org: Ola Electric is a hardware+software product company that operates nothing like a ride-hailing marketplace. PMs must recognise when a new business line requires a genuinely separate product team with different skill sets.

Driver economics determine marketplace health: When driver earnings fall below a viable threshold, supply drops and the consumer experience degrades. PMs running two-sided marketplaces must monitor supply-side health metrics (driver earnings, hours online) as leading indicators of customer experience.

name: Rapido
tagline: The most affordable bike taxi app in India
founders: Aravind Sanka, Rishikesh SR, Pavan Guntupalli
problem: Auto-rickshaws and cabs were too expensive and slow for short-distance urban commutes in Indian cities. Millions of daily commuters — office workers, students, gig workers — needed an affordable, fast, last-mile connectivity option that could navigate congested city streets quickly.
keyFeatures: Bike taxi service with trained captains (riders)
Auto booking at metered fares in major cities
Cab service at competitive pricing
Rapido for Business — corporate mobility solutions
businessModel: Commission from ride fare, subscription plans for Rapido Captains (driver-partners), and B2B corporate accounts
growthStory: Rapido pioneered the bike taxi category in India, initially facing regulatory pushback in multiple states before state governments began permitting bike taxis. It outlasted several bike-taxi competitors and raised $200M+ from Swiggy and others. By 2024, Rapido had expanded into autos and cabs, becoming a full-service mobility platform. It dominates bike taxis in Bengaluru, Hyderabad, and other south Indian cities where two-wheelers are the dominant commute vehicle.
pmLessons: Regulatory ambiguity is not a reason to not build: Rapido launched bike taxis before clear state regulations existed and proactively worked with state transport departments to legalise the category. PMs in regulated spaces can shape policy by demonstrating consumer demand at scale.

Price point unlocks an entirely different TAM: At ₹30-50 for a 3-5 km ride, Rapido serves users who would never pay ₹100 for an auto. This price-sensitive cohort is massive in India and represents an underserved product opportunity in every vertical.

Supply-side product design is as important as the consumer app: Rapido's Captain app is optimised for low-end Android phones with intermittent data, vernacular language support, and simple earnings tracking. PMs building marketplace supply-side products for Bharat must design for constrained devices, not flagships.

name: Urban Company
tagline: Professional home services, delivered with trust
founders: Abhiraj Bhal, Varun Khaitan, Raghav Chandra
problem: Finding trustworthy, skilled professionals for home services — plumbing, cleaning, beauty treatments at home — was unreliable in India. Local handymen had no accountability, pricing was opaque, and quality was inconsistent. Homeowners, especially women booking beauty services, needed safety guarantees and standardised quality.
keyFeatures: Verified and trained professionals for 50+ home service categories
At-home beauty and salon services for women and men
Appliance repair with fixed, upfront pricing
Urban Company Pro — tools and training for service professionals
businessModel: Commission from professionals on each booking, subscription plans for service partners, and premium materials/products sold through service visits
growthStory: Originally launched as UrbanClap in 2014, the company rebranded to Urban Company in 2020 to signal its global ambitions. It expanded to the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Singapore, and Australia. Its model of training and certifying independent professionals — giving them tools, uniforms, and quality standards — created a trusted brand in an inherently fragmented market. Raised $255M and achieved unicorn status at $2.1B valuation in 2021.
keyMetrics: 50,000+ trained service professionals on the platform
Operations in 50+ cities across India, UAE, Singapore, Australia
90%+ customer repeat rate for beauty services
pmLessons: Training supply as a product investment: Urban Company's decision to certify, train, and equip professionals cost money upfront but created a quality standard that differentiated them from generic directories. PMs running services marketplaces must invest in supply-side quality as a core product feature.

Safety is not a feature — it's the category precondition: For home services in India, especially beauty services booked by women, safety verification of professionals is the #1 purchase barrier. PMs must treat trust and safety mechanisms as table-stakes before any other feature.

Fixed pricing eliminates negotiation anxiety: Urban Company's upfront, fixed prices removed the uncomfortable haggling that characterises informal home services in India. In a market where negotiation is culturally embedded, transparent pricing is a UX differentiator.

name: Policybazaar
tagline: Compare and buy the right insurance online
founders: Yashish Dahiya, Alok Bansal, Avaneesh Nirjar
problem: India has one of the world's lowest insurance penetration rates. Consumers had no way to compare plans across insurers, agents pushed high-commission products rather than best-fit policies, and the purchase process was opaque, paper-heavy, and offline-only.
keyFeatures: Side-by-side insurance plan comparison across 50+ insurers
Online policy purchase and instant digital issuance
Claim assistance and grievance redressal support
Paisabazaar — sister platform for loans and credit cards
businessModel: Commission from insurance companies per policy sold (lead generation and sales model), Paisabazaar earns referral fees from lending partners
growthStory: Founded in 2008, Policybazaar went public in November 2021 raising ₹5,625 crore. It democratised insurance by making comparison transparent and eliminating the information asymmetry between agents and buyers. Its TV advertising campaigns featuring actor Akshay Kumar reached crores of Indians with the message of financial responsibility. It captures about 90% of India's online insurance comparison market.
keyMetrics: 90%+ market share of online insurance comparison in India
Listed on NSE at ₹5,625 crore IPO (2021)
Policies from 50+ insurance companies available on platform
pmLessons: Comparison engines are most powerful in opaque, high-consideration markets: Insurance in India was a classic 'lemons market' where agents had information advantages. Policybazaar's PM insight was that radical transparency in pricing and plan comparison would itself be the product differentiation.

Education before conversion: Indian insurance buyers need to understand why they need a policy before choosing one. Policybazaar's content strategy — articles, calculators, and explainer videos — reduced the education burden on sales while increasing intent quality. PMs in fintech should budget for content as a product investment.

Network effects in aggregation: More users → more data on pricing → better recommendations → more users. PMs building aggregators should model how data accumulation creates compounding accuracy advantages over time.

name: MakeMyTrip
tagline: India's largest online travel marketplace
founders: Deep Kalra
problem: Booking flights, hotels, and holiday packages in India required travel agents, phone calls, and offline visits. There was no transparent, self-serve platform where Indians could compare fares, check availability, and book end-to-end travel online with guaranteed confirmation.
keyFeatures: Flight, hotel, bus, and train booking on one platform
Holiday packages with customizable itineraries
myBiz — B2B corporate travel management platform
MakeMyTrip Holidays with curated international packages
businessModel: Booking commissions from airlines and hotels, package margins, myBiz subscription and transaction fees, and advertising from travel brands
growthStory: Founded in 2000 for India-US NRI travel, MakeMyTrip pivoted to domestic Indian travel in 2005 and rapidly became India's dominant OTA. Listed on NASDAQ in 2010. Acquired rival Goibibo in 2016 and ibibo Hotels (redBus included) for ₹4,700 crore, consolidating the Indian OTA market. The COVID-19 pandemic devastated travel in 2020-21 but MakeMyTrip rebounded strongly with pent-up travel demand driving record bookings in FY23-24.
keyMetrics: 18+ million hotels and properties listed globally
pmLessons: Consolidation beats competition in winner-take-most OTA markets: Acquiring Goibibo rather than fighting for years over market share accelerated MakeMyTrip's dominance. PMs should evaluate strategic M&A against prolonged competitive battles when network effects favour consolidation.

B2B adds stability to B2C seasonality: myBiz's corporate travel revenue provides a stable base against leisure travel seasonality. PMs in travel should consider B2B products as a hedge against demand volatility in consumer-facing businesses.

Post-COVID travel surge required proactive capacity planning: MakeMyTrip's systems had to handle unprecedented spike in demand post-lockdown. PMs must model macro demand scenarios and ensure platform scalability is built ahead of anticipated growth events.

name: BigBasket
tagline: India's largest online grocery supermarket
founders: Hari Menon, V.S. Sudhakar, Vipul Parekh, Abhinay Choudhari, Rajesh Yabaji
problem: Indians spent enormous time visiting multiple kirana stores and vegetable vendors for weekly grocery shopping. There was no reliable, large-inventory online alternative that could deliver fresh produce, packaged food, and household items with the reliability and freshness of a physical supermarket.
keyFeatures: Scheduled delivery slots for large grocery orders
BigBasket Private Label brands (BB Royal, Fresho) at value pricing
BB Instant — 10-30 minute delivery via dark stores
Fresh produce sourced directly from farmers
businessModel: Product margin on grocery sales, higher margins on private label products, delivery fees, and subscription plans for free delivery
growthStory: BigBasket pioneered scheduled grocery delivery in India, building massive cold chain and warehousing infrastructure. Acquired by Tata Digital in 2021 for ~$1.3B as part of Tata's digital super-app strategy. The Tata backing gave BigBasket access to distribution through Tata Neu app and enterprise credibility. It now competes in quick commerce via BB Instant against Blinkit and Zepto while maintaining its stronghold in large-basket weekly grocery orders.
keyMetrics: Acquired by Tata Digital at ~$1.3 billion valuation (2021)
Private label products contribute 35%+ of revenue
pmLessons: Private label is a profitability lever disguised as a product category: BB Royal and Fresho command 40-60% margins vs 10-20% on national brands. PMs in e-grocery should map which categories have weak brand loyalty and aggressively develop private label alternatives.

Freshness is a product specification, not a delivery promise: BigBasket's investment in cold chain, temperature-controlled warehouses, and same-day produce procurement from farms is what 'fresh produce delivery' means operationally. PMs must translate consumer promises into physical infrastructure requirements.

Scheduled delivery vs instant delivery serves different use cases: BigBasket's planned weekly shops are complementary to Zepto/Blinkit's urgent fills. PMs should resist the temptation to merge both modes — they optimise for fundamentally different consumer jobs-to-be-done.

name: Blinkit
tagline: Grocery delivery in 10 minutes
founders: Albinder Dhindsa, Saurabh Kumar
problem: Consumers needed groceries and daily essentials immediately — not in hours or even 30 minutes. Traditional grocery delivery models required large basket sizes and planned orders, ignoring the impulse and emergency use cases that represent a significant portion of daily grocery spend.
keyFeatures: 10-minute delivery from neighbourhood dark stores
7,000+ SKUs including groceries, electronics, and medicines
Blinkit for Business — B2B quick commerce service
On-demand medicine and pharmacy delivery
businessModel: Platform fees and delivery fees from consumers, advertising revenue from FMCG brands on-platform, and take rate from merchant partners
growthStory: Originally launched as Grofers in 2013 with a scheduled delivery model, it rebranded to Blinkit in 2021 and pivoted fully to quick commerce. Acquired by Zomato in 2022 for ₹4,447 crore. Under Zomato's umbrella, Blinkit accelerated dark store expansion to 700+ stores and became the quick commerce market leader by GMV. The Grofers-to-Blinkit pivot is one of India's most dramatic product strategy U-turns.
keyMetrics: 12%+ of Zomato's total revenue contribution (Q4 FY24)
pmLessons: Pivoting from scheduled to instant delivery required rebuilding the product entirely: Grofers' warehouse-and-schedule model was fundamentally incompatible with 10-minute delivery. The PM lesson is that sometimes a pivot requires abandoning sunk costs in infrastructure and rebuilding for the new model rather than trying to retrofit.

Dark store placement is a product-market fit variable: A dark store 3 km from demand clusters cannot promise 10 minutes. Blinkit's expansion strategy requires granular demand mapping at pin-code level before each new store opening — PM meets urban geography.

Being acquired can accelerate product vision faster than staying independent: Zomato's balance sheet funded Blinkit's dark store expansion. PMs should evaluate acquisition not as an exit but as a resource injection that can accelerate roadmap delivery.

name: Dream11
tagline: India's biggest fantasy sports platform
founders: Harsh Jain, Bhavit Sheth
problem: Indian cricket fans had no platform to engage with live matches beyond passive watching. Sports viewing lacked active participation, and fans' deep knowledge of player statistics and game dynamics had no outlet for skill-based competition.
keyFeatures: Fantasy cricket, football, kabaddi, and basketball contests
Daily and season-long leagues with cash prizes
Expert team suggestions powered by ML prediction
Live score integration during matches for real-time engagement
businessModel: Platform fee (15-20%) deducted from each contest pool before distributing prizes to winners
growthStory: Dream11 was founded in 2008 and spent a decade educating the market on fantasy sports before the IPL boom supercharged growth. Won a landmark Supreme Court case in 2017 establishing fantasy sports as a 'game of skill' rather than gambling — unlocking ₹thousands of crore in market potential. Became India's first gaming unicorn in 2019. IPL title sponsorship from 2020 gave it a captive audience of 500 million+ cricket viewers. By 2024, it has 220 million+ users.
keyMetrics: 220 million+ registered users (India's largest sports platform)
Active on 13+ sports including cricket, football, kabaddi
IPL title sponsor 2020-2022, generating massive brand awareness
pmLessons: Legal clarity is a product launch prerequisite: Dream11's years of lobbying and litigation to establish fantasy sports as 'skill' rather than 'gambling' were as important as any product feature. PMs in regulated grey areas must invest in policy and legal advocacy as a product function.

Riding a sporting calendar is free product engagement: IPL matches drive 10x traffic spikes with zero marketing spend. PMs building sports products should design the engagement cycle around the sporting calendar, not generic retention mechanics.

Prize structure determines user behaviour: Contest design (large prize vs many smaller prizes) determines who plays and how strategically they engage. PMs must A/B test contest formats as carefully as they test UI features, since contest math determines user satisfaction.

name: ShareChat
tagline: India's own social media in your language
founders: Ankush Sachdeva, Bhanu Pratap Singh, Farid Ahsan
problem: India's 500+ million internet users outside metros speak Hindi, Tamil, Telugu, Marathi, Punjabi, and dozens of other languages — but global social platforms were English-first and culturally tone-deaf to Indian content consumption patterns. Bharat users had no native social media.
keyFeatures: Content feeds in 15 Indian languages with vernacular AI ranking
Moj — short video platform competing with Instagram Reels
ShareChat Live for vernacular live streaming
Creator monetisation tools for regional content creators
businessModel: Advertising from brands targeting regional audiences, creator fund distribution, live gifting revenue share, and performance marketing tools
growthStory: Founded in 2015 by IIT Kanpur alumni, ShareChat grew explosively after TikTok's ban in India in June 2020, with its Moj short video app absorbing a large portion of displaced TikTok users. Raised $145M from Twitter and $300M from Google. By 2022, Moj had 200 million monthly users. The company faced a downturn in 2023 with significant layoffs as ad revenue pressures mounted, reflecting the difficult monetisation landscape for vernacular social media.
keyMetrics: ShareChat + Moj: 300+ million monthly active users combined
Backed by Twitter, Google, Temasek, and Snap
pmLessons: Vernacular is not a localisation project — it is a product architecture decision: Building for 15 languages requires language-specific content ranking models, different content moderation policies, and culturally specific creator tools. PMs cannot simply translate an English product for Bharat users.

Regulatory tailwinds (TikTok ban) can create overnight growth that the product must be ready for: Moj was operationally ready to absorb TikTok's user base when the ban happened. PMs should maintain surge readiness plans for category-disrupting regulatory events.

Ad monetisation for Bharat users is structurally harder: CPMs for Hindi/Tamil content are 3-5x lower than English content globally. PMs building Bharat social platforms must plan for lower per-user revenue and require a larger user base to achieve the same revenue as metro-focused platforms.

name: IndiaMart
tagline: India's largest B2B marketplace connecting buyers and suppliers
founders: Dinesh Agarwal, Brijesh Agrawal
problem: India's 63 million small and medium businesses had no affordable, scalable way to reach national buyers. Physical trade fairs and local directories were the only discovery mechanisms, limiting MSMEs to local markets and making supply chain sourcing inefficient for businesses across India.
keyFeatures: Supplier directory with 100+ million product listings
Lead management dashboard for suppliers
IndiaMart verified supplier certification program
Mobile-first catalogue management for small sellers
businessModel: Subscription fees from suppliers (Silver, Gold, Platinum tiers for lead visibility), premium listing fees, and advertising revenue
growthStory: IndiaMart was founded in 1999 as an internet directory and survived the dot-com bust by focusing relentlessly on SME supplier subscriptions. Listed on NSE in 2019 with an IPO of ₹475 crore. It has remained profitable through its subscription model, a rarity among Indian internet companies. Serves as the backbone of India's MSME supply chain, connecting factories in tier-2 cities with buyers across the country.
keyMetrics: Listed on NSE in 2019 with ₹475 crore IPO
Over 100 million product listings on platform
pmLessons: Solving for price-sensitive MSMEs requires a freemium ladder: IndiaMart's free basic listing with paid upgrades for lead priority mirrors SaaS freemium thinking but for offline-first Indian businesses. PMs must design clear value escalation from free to paid that maps to supplier revenue outcomes.

For B2B products, trust signals matter more than UI: IndiaMart's supplier verification badges, review counts, and company age display are more important conversion drivers than design polish. PMs building B2B products should prioritise trust architecture over visual aesthetics.

Longevity in a market is itself a product moat: IndiaMart's 25 years of supplier and buyer data creates a matching quality advantage that cannot be replicated by a new entrant. PMs should identify how data accumulation creates defensible compounding advantages over time.

name: Khatabook
tagline: Digital khata for every kirana store in India
founders: Ravish Naresh, Dhanesh Kumar, Jaideep Poonia, Vaibhav Kalpe
problem: India's 60+ million small business owners — kirana stores, vendors, traders — maintained credit (udhaar) records in physical ledger books. These were prone to loss, disputes, and offered no digital payment integration. There was no simple, vernacular tool to digitise the centuries-old practice of udhaar ledger-keeping.
keyFeatures: Digital ledger for recording customer credit (udhaar) in local language
WhatsApp-based automated payment reminders to customers
POS and invoicing tools for retail businesses
Business insights and cash flow analytics
businessModel: Freemium model: free core ledger, paid premium features (bulk reminders, advanced analytics), financial services distribution (lending, insurance) to business owners
growthStory: Khatabook raised $60M at a $300M valuation in 2020 and grew to 10 million+ businesses within two years by digitising the ubiquitous udhaar ledger. It competed closely with OkCredit and navigated the thin line between free utility and paid business tools. The company pivoted toward SME fintech — offering working capital loans and neo-banking — to monetise the transaction data captured in its ledger.
keyMetrics: Presence in 750,000+ pin codes across India
pmLessons: Digitising an existing offline behaviour is faster than creating a new one: Khatabook didn't invent udhaar — it digitised it. PMs building for Bharat should identify deeply ingrained offline workflows (ledgers, word-of-mouth, cash) and build digital equivalents rather than forcing new behaviours.

WhatsApp as a product surface: Automated WhatsApp reminders for payment recovery were more effective than in-app nudges because kirana owners' customers were on WhatsApp, not the Khatabook app. PMs must meet users where they already are, not where the product wants them to be.

Data from daily operations is the fintech wedge: Khatabook's ledger data (who owes what, repayment history) is credit bureau data in disguise. PMs should identify what behavioural or transactional data their core product generates and how it can power financial services offerings.

name: OkCredit
tagline: Credit management app for small business owners
founders: Harsh Pokharna, Gaurav Kumar, Aditya Prasad
problem: Small shopkeepers and traders in India lost significant revenue due to untracked customer credit. Paper ledgers were easily lost or disputed, and there was no automated way to remind customers of outstanding dues — leading to bad debt and cash flow problems for millions of micro-businesses.
keyFeatures: Digital credit ledger for tracking customer dues in Hindi and regional languages
Automated SMS and WhatsApp payment reminders
Payment collection link sharing via any messaging platform
Business report generation for GST and tax filing
businessModel: Freemium: free core ledger with premium advanced analytics and bulk reminder features; financial services partnerships for SME lending and insurance
growthStory: OkCredit raised $67M from SoftBank and Tiger Global in 2019 at a $500M valuation. Like Khatabook, it grew by digitising udhaar ledgers for kirana stores and small traders. OkCredit focused strongly on vernacular-first design with support for 13 languages. The company went through a restructuring in 2022 amid the broader funding winter, highlighting the challenges of monetising a free utility product at scale.
keyMetrics: Active in 2,800+ cities and towns across India
pmLessons: Category creation leads to winner-take-most dynamics: OkCredit and Khatabook created the digital udhaar category simultaneously, leading to intense parallel growth. PMs should anticipate that defining a new category often attracts well-funded copycats within months.

Monetisation timing is critical in utility products: Introducing paid features too early breaks the free habit; too late leaves revenue on the table. OkCredit's struggle to monetise its free ledger product is a lesson in planning the freemium-to-paid journey from day one of product design.

Micro-business PMs must think in terms of cash flow, not revenue: Kirana owners care about how much cash they will collect this week, not MoM revenue growth. PMs building for micro-merchants must frame every feature in terms of the owner's immediate cash-flow impact.

name: CARS24
tagline: Sell and buy used cars in 24 hours
founders: Vikram Chopra, Mehul Agrawal, Ruchit Agarwal, Gajendra Jangid
problem: Selling a used car in India was a painful, multi-week process involving brokers, price uncertainty, document fraud risks, and incomplete title transfers. Buyers and sellers were exploited by unorganised dealers with no price transparency or quality assurance.
keyFeatures: Instant car valuation and guaranteed same-day purchase
Certified pre-owned cars with inspection reports for buyers
CARS24 Financial Services for used car loans
International expansion with localised pricing models in UAE, Australia, Thailand
businessModel: Spread between buy price and sell price on owned inventory, auction platform fees for dealer network, financial services revenue (used car loans), and subscription plans for dealer partners
growthStory: CARS24 started as a pure car-buying platform (from consumers) and evolved into a full-stack used car marketplace. It reached unicorn status in 2021 at $1.84B valuation. The company expanded to the UAE, Australia, Thailand, and Malaysia. Its CARS24 Financial Services arm offers used car loans, creating a fintech layer on top of the automotive marketplace. Competes with Spinny and CarDekho in India's $25B used car market.
keyMetrics: International operations in UAE, Australia, Thailand, Malaysia
1 million+ cars bought and sold since inception
pmLessons: Guaranteed pricing eliminates the biggest consumer anxiety: In a market defined by price uncertainty, CARS24's upfront, non-negotiable offer converted hesitant sellers because it removed the fear of being underpaid. PMs in high-uncertainty markets should identify the #1 anxiety and design product features that make it disappear.

Fintech on top of a marketplace amplifies margins: CARS24's car loan offering captures a second revenue stream from the same transaction. PMs building transactional marketplaces should map every adjacent financial service (insurance, loan, warranty) that can be embedded in the transaction flow.

International expansion requires localised pricing models, not just localised UI: CARS24's UAE product had to account for different car age distributions, inspection standards, and buyer preferences. PMs expanding into new geographies must treat pricing strategy as a localisation challenge, not just language.

name: Spinny
tagline: Buy and sell trusted used cars with a 5-day return guarantee
founders: Niraj Singh, Ramanshu Mahaur, Mohit Gupta
problem: Buyers of used cars in India faced high uncertainty about car history, hidden damage, and mechanical issues. The used car market was dominated by untrustworthy dealers and private sellers, making the purchase of a ₹5-10 lakh used car an extremely high-risk, high-anxiety decision.
keyFeatures: 200-point car inspection and quality certification
5-day no-questions-asked return policy
Spinny Assured — price guarantee for sellers
Spinny Care — post-purchase service and maintenance packages
businessModel: Spread on used car transactions (own inventory model), warranty and insurance upsells, Spinny Care maintenance subscriptions, and financing partnerships
growthStory: Spinny distinguished itself from CARS24 by focusing on the buyer experience and trust through its 200-point inspection and 5-day return policy — a radical guarantee in India's used car market. Raised $283M in FY2022 at a $1.8B valuation with Virat Kohli as brand ambassador. Operates a full-stack model owning inventory, refurbishment, and delivery, enabling quality control across the customer experience.
keyMetrics: 15,000+ cars in inventory across hubs in 30+ cities
200-point inspection process for every listed vehicle
5-day return policy accepted industry-wide as a standard
pmLessons: Return policies are trust features, not operational costs: Spinny's 5-day return guarantee dramatically reduced purchase anxiety and increased conversion. The operational cost of processing returns is outweighed by the conversions it enables. PMs should quantify the conversion uplift of trust features before cutting them for cost reasons.

Quality certification is a content strategy: Spinny's 200-point inspection report is both a trust document and a product page. In categories where consumers cannot easily evaluate quality, transparent inspection data is the primary conversion driver.

Brand ambassador choice shapes product perception: Virat Kohli's association positioned Spinny as aspirational within the mass-premium used car segment. PMs managing consumer brand products in India should think carefully about which public figures embody the product's values for the target demographic.

name: Tata 1mg
tagline: India's most trusted health platform for medicines, tests, and consultations
founders: Prashant Tandon, Gaurav Agarwal, Vikas Chauhan
problem: Patients in India had to physically visit pharmacies for medicines with no alternative or home delivery option. There was no platform to compare medicine prices, understand drug interactions, book diagnostic tests, or access transparent health information — especially for patients managing chronic conditions.
keyFeatures: Online pharmacy with 60,000+ medicines and home delivery
Lab test booking with home sample collection
Online doctor consultations across specialisations
Medicine information database with interaction checker
businessModel: Product margin on medicine sales, diagnostic test commissions, doctor consultation fees, and subscription plans (1mg Care) for chronic disease management
growthStory: Founded as 1mg in 2012 and acquired by Tata Digital in 2021 for ~$450M. As part of the Tata Digital ecosystem, 1mg gained access to Tata Neu super-app traffic and enterprise credibility. India's ₹1.8 lakh crore pharma retail market is rapidly shifting online post-COVID, with 1mg positioned as the trusted, doctor-grade health platform. The Tata brand removed trust barriers for ordering prescription medicines online.
keyMetrics: Acquired by Tata Digital for ~$450 million (2021)
Present in 1,000+ cities for medicine delivery
pmLessons: Trust is a compounding asset in health: Patients choose health platforms with extreme conservatism — a single wrong medicine delivery or lab result error can destroy trust permanently. PMs in health must set defect rates 10x lower than in non-health categories and invest in verification systems accordingly.

Chronic disease management is the highest LTV cohort: A diabetic patient ordering insulin, test strips, and quarterly HbA1c tests is a multi-year customer with predictable, high-frequency spending. PMs should build dedicated features for chronic condition cohorts — medication schedules, refill reminders, test tracking.

Government regulation of e-pharmacies creates both risk and moat: India's evolving e-pharmacy regulations are complex and can overnight change what products can be listed. PMs must build regulatory compliance into product architecture, not treat it as an operations problem.

name: Practo
tagline: Your complete health partner — find doctors, book tests, and manage health
founders: Shashank ND, Abhinav Lal
problem: Finding the right doctor in India was word-of-mouth driven and unreliable. Patients had no way to see doctor reviews, qualifications, availability, or fee structures before visiting. Medical record management was paper-based and scattered across different hospitals and clinics with no unified health history.
keyFeatures: Doctor discovery and appointment booking with ratings and reviews
Online consultations via video, audio, and chat
Practo Ray — clinic management and EHR software for doctors
Health record storage and unified medical history
businessModel: SaaS subscription fees from doctors and clinics for Practo Ray, booking fees and lead generation from online appointment marketplace, and telemedicine consultation fees
growthStory: Started as a clinic management SaaS (Practo Ray), expanded into consumer-facing doctor discovery, creating a two-sided marketplace. Expanded aggressively to Southeast Asia and Brazil. Raised $180M from Tencent and others. Faced significant monetisation challenges in the consumer marketplace and underwent multiple rounds of restructuring and layoffs. Practo's story is a cautionary tale about expanding too quickly across geographies before achieving unit economics in the core market.
keyMetrics: Present in 15+ countries including Singapore, Indonesia, Philippines
pmLessons: B2B SaaS and B2C marketplace are fundamentally different product organisations: Practo Ray (clinic SaaS) and Practo consumer (doctor discovery) required different sales motions, pricing, and product teams. PMs must resist the temptation to merge two distinct business models under one product strategy.

Geographic expansion before unit economics breaks the model: Practo's expansion to 15+ countries while struggling to monetise India diluted focus and burned capital without achieving dominance anywhere. PMs must achieve strong retention and unit economics in the primary market before expanding.

Doctor supply quality determines consumer experience: Unverified or inactive doctors on the platform degrade search quality for patients. PMs building health marketplaces must invest heavily in supply curation and activity incentives, not just supply volume.

name: HealthifyMe
tagline: AI-powered health and fitness coaching for Indians
founders: Tushar Vashisht, Mathew Cherian, Sachin Shenoy
problem: Indians needed personalised diet and fitness guidance that accounted for local food habits (idli, dal, sabzi), regional dietary variations, and cultural eating patterns. Western fitness apps used calorie databases irrelevant to Indian food and gym-centric fitness plans unsuitable for most Indian lifestyles.
keyFeatures: AI nutrition coach (Ria) with Indian food calorie database of 1M+ dishes
Personalised diet plans accounting for Indian dietary patterns
Human nutritionist and fitness coach sessions via app
Smart scale and IoT integration for body composition tracking
businessModel: Freemium with paid coach subscriptions (Elite plan at ₹5,000-15,000/year), smart scale hardware sales, and B2B corporate wellness programs
growthStory: HealthifyMe started in 2012 and built India's largest food calorie database covering regional dishes from across India. Raised $75M in 2021. Its AI coach Ria handles 90% of queries before escalating to human coaches, enabling a scalable hybrid model. Expanded to Southeast Asia and the Middle East. Became profitable in FY23 — a rare achievement in the health-tech category. Serves 35 million+ users across 300+ cities.
keyMetrics: 35 million+ registered users across India and SE Asia
Indian food database with 1 million+ local dishes
pmLessons: India-specific data assets are sustainable moats: HealthifyMe's 1 million+ Indian food database was built over years and cannot be quickly replicated by a Western competitor entering India. PMs should invest in India-specific data assets as defensible, long-term competitive advantages.

Hybrid AI+human models scale better than pure human models in price-sensitive markets: At ₹5,000/year, HealthifyMe can afford human coaches only because AI handles 90% of queries. PMs designing coaching products must model the human-to-AI escalation ratio to keep unit economics viable.

Localisation is a product differentiator, not just translation: Knowing that a plate of biryani has ~600 calories, or that Ramadan changes eating patterns for users in certain geographies, is product depth that builds trust with local users. PMs must invest in cultural and culinary localisation as a core feature.

name: Cult.fit
tagline: India's fitness and wellness platform — gyms, yoga, diet, and mental wellness
founders: Mukesh Bansal, Ankit Nagori
problem: Indians had access to traditional gyms but lacked structured, instructor-led fitness programs, mental wellness support, and healthy food delivery integrated in a single subscription. The fitness market was unorganised, with gyms offering no tech or digital engagement beyond physical access.
keyFeatures: Cult centres (physical gyms) with group workout classes and certified trainers
CureFit (renamed) online workout classes and live streaming
EatFit — healthy, calorie-counted meal delivery
MindFit — mental wellness and meditation content
businessModel: Cult Pass memberships (monthly/annual) for physical and online classes, EatFit food delivery margins, and B2B corporate wellness contracts
growthStory: Founded by ex-Myntra CEO Mukesh Bansal, Cult.fit raised $400M and acquired multiple fitness startups. The COVID pandemic collapsed physical gym revenue but accelerated online class adoption. Cult.fit rebranded from CureFit in 2022. It has been rationalising its physical centre network after over-expansion during the growth phase. Backed by Accel, Temasek, and Reckitt, it operates 300+ Cult centres across 20 cities.
keyMetrics: 300+ physical Cult centres across 20 Indian cities
$400 million raised from Accel, Temasek, and others
EatFit serving 50,000+ meals daily at peak
pmLessons: Physical footprint is a product variable, not just a real estate decision: Every Cult centre location is a demand-generation node and a community anchor. PMs building offline-online hybrid products must model physical locations as customer acquisition cost centres with their own LTV calculations.

Super-app bundling works when verticals share a user persona: Fitness, food, and mental wellness all serve the 'health-conscious urban Indian' persona. Bundling them increases LTV per user but requires that each vertical delivers standalone value — weak verticals drag down the whole bundle.

COVID showed that physical product businesses need a digital bridge: Cult.fit's online classes kept subscriber relationships alive during lockdowns. PMs building physical-first products must always maintain a digital engagement layer that can substitute for physical access during disruptions.

name: BYJU'S
tagline: The world's most loved learning app
founders: Byju Raveendran, Divya Gokulnath
problem: Indian students preparing for competitive exams (JEE, NEET, UPSC) relied on rote-learning-heavy coaching institutes that were geographically limited to cities like Kota. Quality exam preparation was inaccessible to students in smaller towns and was prohibitively expensive through physical coaching.
keyFeatures: Video-based, visual learning content for K-12 and competitive exams
Adaptive learning paths personalised by student performance
BYJU'S Tuition Centre — physical learning centres for hybrid model
Acquisitions: Aakash (test prep), WhiteHat Jr (coding), Great Learning
businessModel: Course subscriptions (₹10,000-35,000/year), Aakash Institute fees, online tuition fees, and device-bundled course packages
growthStory: BYJU'S became the world's most valued edtech startup at $22B in 2022, fuelled by aggressive sales tactics and massive pandemic-era demand. The company made 15+ acquisitions globally and spent heavily on IPL sponsorships and the FIFA World Cup jersey sponsorship. However, audit concerns, alleged financial irregularities, and a collapse of the edtech segment post-COVID led to an unprecedented downfall. By 2023-24, it faced insolvency proceedings — a dramatic cautionary tale in startup history.
keyMetrics: Peak valuation of $22 billion (2022) — world's most valued edtech
pmLessons: Aggressive sales without product-market fit retention is unsustainable: BYJU'S high-pressure door-to-door sales generated enormous revenue but with extremely poor course completion rates. PMs must insist that sales and product alignment means customers actually use and value the product — vanity metrics hide retention failures.

Post-pandemic demand normalisation is a product planning risk: EdTech's COVID boom was not a permanent demand shift. PMs must build financial models with demand scenarios that include post-crisis normalisation, not assume pandemic-era growth rates will persist.

Acquisition integration is a product management function: BYJU'S 15+ acquisitions were rarely integrated into a coherent product experience. PMs must own integration planning — not just legal M&A — ensuring acquired products enhance rather than fragment the core product ecosystem.

name: Unacademy
tagline: India's largest learning platform for competitive exams
founders: Gaurav Munjal, Roman Saini, Hemesh Singh
problem: India has over 10 million students preparing for competitive exams like UPSC, JEE, NEET, and CAT every year, but quality educators are scarce and concentrated in physical coaching hubs. Most students had no access to India's best teachers regardless of how hard they studied.
keyFeatures: Live and recorded classes from top educators for 100+ exams
Unacademy Subscription for unlimited access
Combat — live quiz battles for exam practice
Offline Unacademy Centres for hybrid learning
businessModel: Subscription fees (Unacademy Plus) for unlimited course access, Unacademy Centre fees for physical coaching, and revenue sharing with top educators (Educator Program)
growthStory: Started as a YouTube channel by Roman Saini (ex-IAS officer), Unacademy became a platform allowing any educator to teach and earn. Raised $440M and reached a $3.4B valuation in 2021. The Educator Program gave India's best teachers the ability to earn ₹1 crore+ annually, attracting top talent from physical coaching institutes. Post-COVID demand normalisation hit Unacademy hard with multiple rounds of layoffs in 2022-23. The company refocused on its strongest exam categories.
keyMetrics: Top educators earning ₹1 crore+ annually on the platform
pmLessons: Creator economics determine supply quality on ed platforms: Unacademy's decision to share significant revenue with educators (some earning ₹1 crore+/year) was a supply-side product decision that attracted India's best teachers away from physical institutes. PMs building creator marketplaces must model educator economics as carefully as learner UX.

Live classes outperform recorded content for competitive exams: The exam preparation use case demands real-time doubt resolution and peer competition — elements that recorded video cannot replicate. PMs must match product format to the specific learning job-to-be-done, not default to asynchronous video.

Exam-specific cohorts are better product units than 'learners': UPSC aspirants and JEE students have completely different schedules, content needs, and emotional states. PMs should build exam-specific products within the platform rather than building a single generic learner experience.

name: upGrad
tagline: Higher education and upskilling for working professionals
founders: Ronnie Screwvala, Mayank Kumar, Phalgun Kompalli, Ravijot Chugh
problem: Working professionals in India seeking to upskill for data science, management, technology, and digital marketing roles had limited access to affordable, reputable, university-backed online programs. Traditional MBA/engineering colleges had no online equivalent that combined course quality with job placement support.
keyFeatures: Online PG programs in partnership with top universities (BITS, IIIT-B, Liverpool)
Career support with job placement assistance and interview coaching
Upskilling for working professionals with weekend-compatible schedules
upGrad Campus — campus recruitment and hiring platform for recruiters
businessModel: Course fees (₹1-10 lakh per program), career services bundled into premium programs, corporate upskilling contracts, and recruitment platform fees
growthStory: Founded by Bollywood producer turned entrepreneur Ronnie Screwvala, upGrad focused on higher education (PG diploma, MBA equivalent) rather than K-12. It reached a $2.25B valuation in 2021 and acquired multiple companies including Harappa Education, IMTLD, and Volcanics. Unlike K-12 edtech, upGrad's professional education thesis proved more resilient post-COVID as working professionals continued investing in career development.
keyMetrics: 500+ hiring partners including Google, Amazon, Deloitte
Programs offered in partnership with 50+ universities
pmLessons: Working professionals require job outcome guarantees, not just content: upGrad's job placement assistance is not a feature — it is the core value proposition. In professional education, the product is the career outcome, not the course. PMs must design placement support as a first-class product, not an afterthought.

University partnerships create institutional credibility that pure online brands cannot: A degree or certificate from BITS Pilani via upGrad is more trusted by Indian employers than a purely online brand certificate. PMs should identify institutional partnerships that transfer credibility to their product.

Adult learners have low completion rates — design for accountability: Working professionals face competing priorities. upGrad's cohort-based learning, mentors, and peer groups are product features that fight completion drop-off. PMs must design accountability mechanisms for adult learners who lack institutional pressure.

name: Vedantu
tagline: Live online tutoring from India's best teachers
founders: Vamsi Krishna, Pulkit Jain, Saurabh Saxena, Anand Prakash
problem: Students in small towns and rural India preparing for JEE, NEET, and board exams had no access to qualified teachers. Physical coaching centres were concentrated in a few cities. Parents spent lakhs on subpar local tutors or sent children away to coaching hubs, creating financial and emotional strain.
keyFeatures: Live interactive classes with real-time doubt resolution by top IITian teachers
WAVE — Vedantu's proprietary whiteboard and class engagement platform
Free live classes on YouTube for broad reach
Test series and personalised study plans for JEE/NEET preparation
businessModel: Paid subscription courses for live classes, freemium model with free YouTube classes driving paid conversion, and test series packages
growthStory: Vedantu raised $100M in 2020 and achieved unicorn status at $1B valuation in 2021. Its WAVE platform for live interactive classes was a proprietary product moat. Like other edtech companies, it underwent significant layoffs in 2022 (600 employees) as pandemic demand normalised. However, Vedantu focused on its core JEE/NEET segment and maintained profitability targets. Its free YouTube channel with millions of subscribers is a lead generation engine for paid products.
keyMetrics: WAVE platform with 70+ interactive tools for live teaching
pmLessons: Free YouTube as a funnel is a product strategy: Vedantu gives away high-quality free content on YouTube to attract students, then converts them to paid plans. For Indian price-sensitive markets, the freemium-via-YouTube approach reduces CAC dramatically. PMs should treat free content as acquisition infrastructure.

Proprietary classroom technology is a moat: WAVE's 70+ interactive tools for whiteboard drawing, polls, and Q&A give Vedantu's live classes a quality edge over Zoom-based alternatives. PMs in edtech should invest in building platform-specific teaching tools rather than relying on generic video conferencing.

Teacher NPS is the product's real quality metric: If the best teachers leave the platform for BYJU'S or physical institutes, course quality collapses. PMs must track teacher satisfaction and earnings as leading indicators of platform quality and student outcomes.

name: Udaan
tagline: B2B trade platform connecting manufacturers, traders, and retailers
founders: Amod Malviya, Sujeet Kumar, Vaibhav Gupta
problem: India's FMCG, pharma, and food supply chains relied on inefficient, cash-heavy, multi-layer distribution networks of brokers and distributors between manufacturers and small retailers. Retailers paid inflated prices, had no credit access, and manufacturers had no visibility into retail inventory.
keyFeatures: B2B marketplace for manufacturers to sell directly to kirana stores and traders
udaan Capital — working capital loans and credit lines for retailers
Category-specific catalogues for FMCG, pharma, fashion, staples
Integrated logistics with warehouse and last-mile delivery network
businessModel: Transaction margin on B2B orders, udaan Capital interest income on working capital loans, and logistics service fees
growthStory: Founded by ex-Flipkart executives, udaan became India's fastest-ever B2B unicorn in 2018, just 26 months after founding. Raised $585M from Lightspeed and others. At peak it facilitated $2B+ in annual GMV. However, the company underwent severe restructuring in 2022-23, laying off 1,000+ employees and pivoting to profitability focus. The unit economics challenge of B2B distribution — thin margins, high logistics cost, credit risk — continues to be the central challenge.
keyMetrics: Unicorn in 26 months — India's fastest B2B unicorn (2018)
3 million+ retailers served on the platform at peak
pmLessons: B2B marketplaces require embedded fintech to solve working capital gaps: Retailers buy on 30-60 day credit from traditional distributors — without udaan Capital's credit offering, retailer adoption would be limited. PMs building B2B trade platforms must plan for embedded financial services as a core product requirement, not an add-on.

FMCG distribution margins are razor-thin: 1-2% margins on commodity FMCG orders make it very hard to build a sustainable GMV-based business. PMs must design B2B marketplace businesses around value-added services (credit, logistics, analytics) rather than purely order volume.

Category focus matters in B2B: FMCG, pharma, and fashion have completely different buyer behaviour, margin structures, and regulatory requirements. PMs launching B2B marketplaces should resist multi-category temptation early and achieve dominance in one vertical before expanding.

name: Delhivery
tagline: India's largest fully integrated logistics platform
founders: Sahil Barua, Mohit Tandon, Bhavesh Manglani, Suraj Saharan, Kapil Bharati
problem: India's logistics was dominated by fragmented state-level transporters with no unified tracking, no reliable delivery timelines, and no technology layer. E-commerce companies like Flipkart and Snapdeal needed a national courier partner with real-time tracking and COD (cash on delivery) handling at scale.
keyFeatures: Express parcel delivery with next-day delivery in 30+ cities
COD (Cash on Delivery) remittance management for e-commerce
Freight services for large shipments and B2B logistics
Delhivery API integration for real-time tracking and shipping management
businessModel: Per-shipment delivery fees (variable by weight, distance, service level), freight forwarding margins, and value-added services (COD handling, returns management)
growthStory: Started as a hyperlocal food delivery startup, Delhivery pivoted to express logistics for e-commerce in 2012. It built India's most sophisticated logistics technology platform and listed on the NSE in May 2022 raising ₹5,235 crore. As India's largest logistics company by shipments, it processes 1.8 million+ shipments daily. Acquired Spoton Logistics in 2022 to strengthen B2B freight capabilities.
keyMetrics: Listed on NSE at ₹5,235 crore IPO (May 2022)
Presence in 18,000+ pin codes across India
pmLessons: Logistics technology is a product differentiator: Delhivery's route optimization, hub planning, and real-time tracking were built in-house over years. In a commoditised industry, technology-led efficiency is the only sustainable moat. PMs in logistics must treat engineering investment in route-optimization as a product feature.

COD management is a fintech problem embedded in logistics: Handling, reconciling, and remitting cash collected on delivery across 18,000 pin codes requires sophisticated financial operations. PMs building e-commerce logistics must design COD as a financial product with reconciliation SLAs, not just a payment collection task.

Pivot early from unscalable to scalable models: Delhivery's hyperlocal food delivery pivot to express logistics was critical. PMs must be willing to abandon early business models when evidence shows a higher-potential direction, even when it means winding down initial product lines.

name: Shiprocket
tagline: Shipping and logistics made simple for D2C brands and SMEs
founders: Saahil Goel, Akshay Ghulati, Vishesh Khurana, Gautam Kapoor
problem: India's small D2C brands and online sellers could not negotiate competitive courier rates like large e-commerce companies. They were stuck with retail courier pricing, multiple shipping partners with different tracking systems, and no unified dashboard for shipping management across Shopify, WooCommerce, and marketplace orders.
keyFeatures: Aggregated shipping across 17+ courier partners with negotiated rates
Unified order management dashboard for multi-channel sellers
Shiprocket Fulfillment — outsourced warehousing and pick-pack for SMEs
